data.title: Air Taxis And Oil Money Mix Well: Initiating Joby Aviation With A Buy
data.description:
Joby Aviation, Inc. (NYSE:JOBY) is not a mainstream name, and that’s because it's working in what is today still a niche market: developing EV takeoff and landing aircraft (eVTOL) with the goal of coming out with an aerial ridesharing service. The company has been around since 2009, but only went public in 2021, since the stock has gone down 25% from $10.71 per share to around $7.88 per share at current levels. The stock is up almost 24% over the past month after signing a memorandum of understanding (MoU) with Abdul Latif Jameel (an eight-decades-old network of diversified businesses) to “explore opportunities to establish a distribution agreement in Saudi Arabia for Joby’s electric aircraft.” We're initiating the stock with a Buy not on this deal, but on the potential growth Joby's network can reel in once aVTOL hits its inflection point.

Joby's talks with Saudi Arabia follow the trend of U.S.-backed companies getting cozy with the oil-rich nation. Nothing is set in stone yet, but the potential is to deliver “up to 200 Joby aircraft and related services valued at approximately $1B are expected by businesses over the coming years.” So, the stock is trading up on the fact that a deal may be coming soon, and if so, it would dramatically reverse Joby's financials, which, as of Q1, still have no revenue to show, as seen below. Not to mention, it would also boost the stock price, which increased roughly 28% between Tuesday and Wednesday of the last week on the Toyota (TM) investment alone and is still digesting the potential of deals with Saudi Arabia.

Joby's in bed with all the right people

For now, we remain in the preliminary phase of this deal and of the broader air taxi sector. But this is a window to buy in on what we believe to be an extremely uniquely-positioned company with clean execution. What’s holding investor confidence together on this deal is the fact that the Saudi investor group already invests in Joby and has close ties with Toyota.

Why does this matter? Toyota Motor Corp is a top shareholder in Joby and announced the investment of $250M in Joby in late May, marking the first half of a $500M commitment. As of Q1, reported in early May, Joby has around $813M in cash and short-term investments, and then the $500M from Toyota on top of that. So, Joby is pretty well-positioned when it comes to reach within the mobility market, which should help secure its go-to-market strategy. This is a big positive because when it comes to new tech, the real struggle is getting commercial adoption and that's what Joby is working on very early on.

So, what we know so far is that Joby is working on getting U.S. Federal Aviation Administration certification, expected in 2H25, and is likely to launch its first commercial flights in the Middle East, but that doesn’t mean it’s ignoring American markets. The company is also partnering with Delta Air Lines (DAL) and Uber (UBER) to prepare the U.S. market for launch, eyeing Los Angeles and New York City as the main launch markets.

We see more attractive entry points at current levels, considering the stock price is erasing some of its gains after the news. The RSI bumped into overbought territory in late May on the Toyota investment before falling to 60, and the stock is up 24% over the past three months, as shown on the chart below against the S&P 500 (SP500). Due to all this momentum, it is no surprise that Joby trades at premium multiples; its Price to Book is 7.27x compared to a group average of 2.90x. Its forward EV/Sales, based on Seeking Alpha’s metrics, is also insane at 2,965.41x compared to a group average of 1.93x. So, is it the right time to buy in?

The thing is, Joby has a strong standing within the very new sector of eVTOL and urban air mobility, estimated to grow at a CAGR of 35.6% between 2024-2028, but this doesn't mean it won't experience hurdles. So while we think this is an attractive long-term pick, we have two specific hurdles we're watching: regulations and commercial adoption. These are two challenges Joby is working on, but that in our opinion investors should be well aware of heading into the stock.

Regulations:

The company is actively working on getting the necessary certification for flight operations from the FAA; on this quarter’s earnings call, management noted that:

I’m pleased to say that despite it being a shorter quarter, we actually beat last quarter’s record, accelerating forward by a full 12 percentage points on the FAA side and moving the Joby side up to 62% complete. This is critically important progress because each plan we sign off with the FAA is a step towards unlocking TIA flights.

The thing investors should keep in mind is that Joby is not the only one working on this. There’s a push from air taxi companies to get approval for air taxis as urban transportation demand expands and hits commercial markets. Vertical Aerospace (EVTL) is one of the other players working on this with its VX4 air taxi. Another is Archer Aviation (ACHR), and the other is Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF), which is extremely well-positioned in the Chinese market, with Bloomberg reporting "China is considering placing an order for hundreds of Airbus SE aircraft as soon as next month." While each of these players is approaching the market from a specific vertical, we think the global market is beginning to get segmented and we're glad Joby is calling dibs on the Middle East.

Joby is still in the testing phase now; it's testing all-electric aircraft that can carry up to four passengers and are constructed for urban air mobility, running up to 200 miles per hour. Earlier in May, the company announced “successfully completing flights with a full transition from vertical to cruise flight, and back again, with a pilot onboard.” Following that, the company announced the success of two simultaneous aircraft during testing for the first time.

More successful testing will ease the road to getting approval; CEO JoeBen Bevirt said that “with six aircraft in our test fleet, flying multiple aircraft at a time will play an important role in accelerating our certification testing and supporting our plans to carry our first passengers next year.” So, Joby is taking all the necessary steps to get the necessary certification, as shown in the roadmap from Q1 below, which would then leave it with its next big challenge: building up for commercial adoption.

Commercial adoption:

From current visibility, it looks like Joby is among the closest to actually seeing its air taxis take off commercially, but closest doesn’t mean close. We think that the market could be getting a bit ahead of itself as there are a lot of factors to take into account for this tech to work, and specifically to work in the climate of Saudi Arabia and/or Dubai, which are heavily prone to sandstorms and high heat levels. The company is eyeing Dubai in addition to Saudi Arabia with intentions to start passenger services by 2026, which is soon; the company’s shareholder letter highlighted that:

IN DUBAI, construction of the vertiport at the International Airport is progressing well and we remain on track to begin pilot-on-board testing in the Emirate mid-year, following the success of our inhabited transition flights in California.

While we do think special precautions need to be taken when testing happens in the Middle East, we don’t think it’s a task Joby can’t handle. The UAE is prepared to help, too; according to UAE civil aviation authority’s issued statement in February, the country is starting to map air corridors for air taxis, noting that "Air corridor mapping for piloted and autonomous air taxis and drones is a crucial milestone that will enable the seamless implementation of advanced air mobility into the UAE's infrastructure."

Joby has already applied for certification that would allow it to operate within the commercial air transport in the UAE since September of last year. This will be a slow-burning candle, with the mapping requiring an estimated 20 months to be defined, but the good news is that the ball is rolling. So, while we do think hurdles and sandstorms and competition may arise, we believe the longer-term runway for Joby’s position within the Middle East is secure.

FYI: The aircraft themselves are produced in Ohio and can deliver up to 500 aircraft per year; this makes Joby’s job of navigating potential tariffs easier, considering the company’s strong commitment to being part of the American innovation force. Bevirt noted that

“Our air taxi is designed, manufactured, and assembled here in the US, and we are a proudly American company, employing engineers and other experts across 40 different US states.”

This is good news because it solidifies the fact that Joby has a strong standing in the U.S., which could help when it comes to military adoption of their aircraft, which we think is an easier market to tap into than urban transportation, considering the higher defense spending coming from the U.S. and NATO.

Bottom Line: There is no revenue yet, meaning it won't be hard to increase $0 sales

Joby is still very much in its R&D phase of existence, so is the entire sector, for that matter. If you take a look at their financials for Q1, this becomes painfully clear. For Q1, Joby’s net loss was $82.4M with $163.3M in net operating loss and $80.9M in other income. The loss is shrinking, but investors shouldn't hold onto that too closely, as higher spend is needed for the next phase. What are now preliminary talks could very likely lead to a monetizable opportunity.

The stock is pricey and has run up on investor confidence in what deals may come with Saudi Arabia. While that is usually a turn-off for us, we think Joby is an exception because it's playing the long game and building up a first-mover advantage in what should be a multi-billion-dollar market.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Meta: AI Agents May Prove To Be A Multi-Billion-Dollar Opportunity
data.description:
Meta Platforms, Inc. (NASDAQ:META) has been my favorite large-cap tech stock in the past few years. Every time the stock plummeted - think of 2018, 2020, and 2022 - I have been brave enough to add to my stake, and I couldn't have been happier with that. Even during periods when Meta seemingly made miscalculated bets, which included doubling down on the metaverse, I had faith in the core business of the company. Today, investors are closely monitoring Meta's AI strategy, given how the company has ramped up capex to pursue a future as an AI-focused tech giant. Upon closer evaluation of Meta's spending on AI agents and the long-term monetization potential of these agents, I am convinced that AI agents present a multi-billion-dollar opportunity for the company.

Meta's AI Agents Are Already Available For Testing

Meta has developed AI agents across a few broad categories. At a broad level, these agents fall into one of the following categories.

  1. Conversational AI assistants
  2. Business AI agents
  3. Creator AI agents
  4. Specialized research AI agents

For the purpose of this analysis, I will only focus on Business AI agents as I believe this is where the biggest monetization opportunity lies, at least in the foreseeable future.

The core functionality of business AI agents is to help businesses interact with customers, automate certain processes, and assist with sales. Some of the key capabilities of these AI agents include responding to customer queries, generating sales leads, and automating ad creation. Meta has already started beta testing some of these features through its business AI bot, which is focused on supporting SMBs in delivering automated customer care. Announcing the pilot testing of business AI on LinkedIn last March, Meta's Head of Business AI Clara Shih wrote:

Today, I’m excited to share a sneak peek of Meta’s work on business AI– agents made easy for every business. Using the brand content you've shared on Instagram, Facebook, and WhatsApp, product catalogs, FAQs, and more, you can easily set up business AIs to respond instantly to customers, free your team from repetitive tasks, and help convert more leads, faster. Just as we did for ads, we want to simplify and democratize powerful AI tools for even the smallest of businesses.

For now, interested business users have to be on a waitlist to gain access to business AI agents developed by Meta. However, the company has pledged to bring these tools to all business users in the future.

2025 May Be The Year Of AI Agents

Meta is not the only company that believes in the massive potential of AI agents. Earlier this year, in a blog post, OpenAI's Sam Altman made a strong case for 2025 being the year in which AI agents come to the spotlight. He wrote:

We believe that, in 2025, we may see the first AI agents “join the workforce” and materially change the output of companies. We continue to believe that iteratively putting great tools in the hands of people leads to great, broadly-distributed outcomes.

Giving a simplified example focused on the introduction of a software engineer AI agent, Sam Altman wrote:

Let’s imagine the case of a software engineering agent, which is an agent that we expect to be particularly important. Imagine that this agent will eventually be capable of doing most things a software engineer at a top company with a few years of experience could do, for tasks up to a couple of days long. It will not have the biggest new ideas, it will require lots of human supervision and direction, and it will be great at some things but surprisingly bad at others. Still, imagine it as a real-but-relatively-junior virtual coworker. Now imagine 1,000 of them. Or 1 million of them. Now imagine such agents in every field of knowledge work.

Meta, which is powering its AI agents with Llama, will be up against the likes of ChatGPT. OpenAI has already explored the idea of AI agents through the Assistants API, which is a tool developed by the AI giant to help product developers utilize its technology to build custom AI assistants that can function as agents. According to The Information, OpenAI is planning to charge close to $20,000 a month for advanced research agents and approximately $2,000 a month for software developer AI agents.

After comparing the Assistants API with Meta's business AI, I believe these tools cater to different end markets.

OpenAI's Assistants API is more focused on businesses that are focused on creating deeply integrated AI agents that are not limited to just a single platform. Meta, on the other hand, targets SMBs that already have a meaningful presence on its platforms (Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp). In this context, Meta is building integrated AI agents that can be easily deployed into the existing ecosystem of a business whereas OpenAI primarily functions as a technology solutions provider for product developers.

Projecting Direct Revenues For Business AI

To get a ballpark idea of the revenue opportunity we are looking at, I did some calculations using conservative assumptions and publicly available data. CEO Mark Zuckerberg has already hinted at monetization in several instances, so it makes sense for investors to take AI agents seriously, especially the business AI product suite. After announcing that Meta AI surpassed one billion monthly active users a few days ago, Zuckerberg commented:

There will be opportunities to either insert paid recommendations or offer a subscription service so that people can pay to use more compute.

For business AI tools, I believe Meta is likely to focus on a subscription-based pricing model although paid recommendations could be a consideration in the early stages. Monetization for business AI will, in my opinion, involve two distinct product categories.

  1. Business messaging monetization, which includes business customers on WhatsApp Business, Facebook Messenger, and Instagram DM.
  2. AI ad automation monetization.

Let's first look at the addressable target market for Meta business AI.

According to Statista, WhatsApp Business had almost 765 million monthly active users as of Q4 2024. The number of active WhatsApp Business users has grown exponentially since 2020.

Exhibit 1: WhatsApp Business MAUs

This, unfortunately, is not an accurate representation of the number of actual businesses that use the platform. I use WhatsApp Business as well, but only because I have two mobile numbers. Since WhatsApp does not allow using two numbers on one device, the easy workaround is to download and use WhatsApp Business for your second number. Based on mid-2024 statistics, around 50 million businesses used the platform at the time. For my projections, I will use this as a baseline.

Facebook Messenger has over a billion MAUs, but the absolute majority of these users are individuals, not businesses. According to some recent estimates, around 40 million businesses use Facebook Messenger monthly. This accounts for around 4% of MAUs and is a more realistic estimate of monthly business users.

Instagram, on the other hand, has over two billion MAUs, and approximately 10% of these are businesses, so we are looking at around 200 million global business users. The rise of influencer marketing in the past decade has forced businesses of every size and scale to join and maintain business profiles on Instagram to reach the younger demographic.

When it comes to AI ad automation, I am focused on Meta's plans to fully automate the ad creation process by the end of 2026. According to Meta's projections, businesses will soon be able to handle the entire ad campaign, from inputting product images to targeting the right demographic market, using AI. In my opinion, Meta will be in a good position to charge a subscription fee from advertisers for these features.

In order to get an idea about the prices Meta would be able to charge businesses for all these AI agents, I looked at the existing pricing models of AI SaaS tools.

  • Salesforce Einstein starts from $75/month.
  • HubSpot AI starts from $20 per user a month.
  • Jasper, a popular content creation tool, starts from $39/month.
  • Popular chatbots developed by Chatfuel, Botsonic, and Tidio start from around $20/month.
  • Currently available lead generation tools such as Apollo and Outreach start from around $49 per user a month.

The next step is to project potential revenues for Meta.

It is important to note that Meta's business AI agents are likely to be able to charge premium prices if these tools prove to be game-changing, enabling businesses to replace agency work with AI agents. However, to err on the side of caution, I have decided to use more conservative assumptions for pricing and adoption to get a general idea of the revenue potential of AI agents.

In a simplified scenario, I believe Meta's target market will be around 110 million businesses for its business AI suite. This includes 40 million WhatsApp Business users, 50 million businesses that use Messenger, and 20 million Instagram business accounts (just 10% of all business accounts). In the initial phase, I expect Meta to capture a 15% share of this target market for its business AI suite which includes AI business messaging features. This gives us 16.5 million paying subscribers.

At an average annual revenue per business of $700, we are looking at $11.5 billion in annual revenue.

The AI ad automation tool is likely to enjoy much higher average annual revenue per business, but at a lower adoption rate in my opinion. Assuming average annual revenue of $1,500 and an adoption rate of just 5% (5.5 million businesses), projected revenue comes to $8.25 billion.

In total, therefore, we are looking at almost a $20 billion revenue opportunity with AI agents in the initial phase of monetization.

While I used a highly simplified scenario, rumored pricing strategies for OpenAI's AI agents suggest Meta may actually end up bringing in a substantially higher revenue from this segment in the long run.

Risks To Monitor

When it comes to AI agents, I believe the biggest risk is the threat of competitors. I will keep a close eye on established AI giants such as OpenAI and Anthropic to identify any adverse movements in the competitive landscape.

In general, investing in Meta carries several other risks, including unfavorable regulatory actions and the possibility that ramped-up capital expenditures on AI deliver lackluster returns in the short run. In addition, given that Meta relies on advertising revenue, the advertising market's sensitivity to global economic growth also poses a risk. Given rising geopolitical tensions and uncertain tax policies, investors should not rule out the possibility of a major slowdown in the global economy. This would negatively impact the global advertising industry.

Takeaway

Meta Platforms is well-positioned to emerge as a big winner of the growing popularity of AI agents. The company is already exploring with a few agents, and there is massive monetization potential when it comes to its business AI suite. Starting next year, I believe we will see Meta focusing on the monetization of its AI product suite given that monthly users have now surpassed one billion (this fits well into Meta's historical playbook). Although Meta may not be cheaply valued at a forward P/E of 26, I continue to believe in the company's potential to grow in double-digits in the next five years.

data.title: B2Gold: Still A Buy, But Gramalote Could Bring Volatility To The Stock
data.description:
During the broader market's selloff driven by the tariff drama earlier this year, I was quite bullish on B2Gold Corp. (NYSE:BTG). Since my last coverage, the stock is up in the high single digits. Not bad, considering a 10-week timeframe.

I have now downgraded my rating to a Buy, as I see a risk building up on the horizon. Is this risk enough to warrant a sell rating?

I believe the answer is no. The growth profile looks quite good this year, considering the start of the Goose and Fekola Regional projects. Management estimates gold production from the Goose Project to be between 120,000 and 150,000 ounces in 2025, with the first gold pour by the end of Q2 2025. At the Fekola Complex in Mali, the first gold production from Fekola Regional is expected around mid 2025, with a projected gold production between 20,000 and 25,000 ounces that year.

Overall, the guidance for this year looks quite favorable, and it all points out that this year the company will recover to pre-2024 (bad year, considering the operational challenges in Mali) gold production levels.

My main concern is the rally in anticipation of the feasibility study results of Gramalote. I believe this could be a sell on the news pattern, although, if the selloff occurs, I believe it will be short-lived as the Goose and Fekola Regional projects will have a positive impact on gold production this year, as shown in the chart above.

Big Catalyst Ahead

This year, investors are paying close attention to three projects. Well, actually four, but the Antelope deposit is so small in comparison (see below chart), so I believe it's not in the spotlight. These three key projects are: the Goose project in Canada, the Fekola Regional project in Mali, and the Gramalote project in Colombia.

Out of the three projects, I believe the one that presents the biggest upside from a price action perspective, and not (emphasis) a near term financial perspective, is Gramalote. Why?

Hype. As I noted in my previous coverage, B2Gold's Gramalote Project in Colombia is in the final stages of its feasibility study. In the Q1 earnings call, management stated that the study is on track to be completed by the end of June. If the results are favorable, I believe we could see a development decision shortly after (i.e. mid July). From the previous PEA assessment released in June 2024, the pre-production capital estimate is around $800 million.

The contingency is only 7.8% of the total pre-production cost. In my view, that's quite low. Just take into consideration the 23% cost overrun for the Goose project in Canada. Overall, I believe the pre-production cost of Gramalote could be in the ballpark of $1 billion. Is this concerning? I believe the answer is no.

I considered including below the initial FCF expectation from the Gramalote PEA assessment. Again, I'd keep a close eye on the upcoming feasibility study for any positive updates on this free cash flow projection, considering the drastic increase in gold's spot price since June 2024 (the date of the PEA assessment) to today's $3,360/oz value.

Finally, here is the economic summary for this mine:

Notice the highlight above on the expected gold spot price in their PEA study for the first three years. Considering that value, the company expects an internal rate of return of 20.6% and a mine payback timeline of 3.1 years. Again, please bear in mind the steep increase in gold's spot price for the past year.

For this reason, I'd keep a close eye on any amendments to the economic summary in the upcoming feasibility study results. Overall, considering the 44% increase in gold's spot price for the past year, I believe the results will be positive, which makes me wonder about the following risk.

Risks

Have a quick look at BTG's chart below.

Do you see the 27% increase since mid May? Do you see the breakout beyond the $3.5 resistance level? I believe this is a clear sign of premature excitement prior to the release of the Gramalote feasibility study. Why do I think so?

The S&P500 is recovering from the tariff selloff. The tariff drama is mostly gone, and I believe most countries will get the universal 10% tariff, except maybe China and other Southeast Asian or East European countries. Uncertainty around trade policy is decreasing and the gold spot price has finally found a ceiling (i.e. resistance) in the $3,300s per ounce. So, why has BTG's share price taken off for the past couple of weeks?

I can't help but wonder if investors are bidding up the share price because they expect good news from the Gramalote study. If you've been in the markets for a while, this has the feel of a classic "sell on the news".

I am worried that the excitement will fade away soon. Why?

If we look at the timeline for Gramalote, the construction permit needs to be modified from the PEA study. This was a topic discussed during the latest Q&A, where I was able to find a rough timeline for this mine.

So that construction permit has to be modified for whatever we've changed in the PEA. We're kind of estimating a 12-month to 15-month period on that. So your answer is absolutely correct. It could be earlier if the government wants to play. But you're right, it's kind of that '28, '29 is probably right. - Bill Lytle

The excerpt above puts into perspective the timeline for Gramalote. We are talking about the end of the decade. Again, I'd keep a close eye on the feasibility study for any concrete dates regarding the start of the project, but it seems to me that the excitement won't last for too long.

Other risks include permitting delays for the Fekola Regional project. Back in September last year, the Malian government approved the extension projects at Fekola. However, the company is still awaiting the consolidated permit.

So that really is the catalyst that allows us to now submit the exploitation application. It's been completed, it's translated, it's ready to go. We need a little bit of time to include all of the details that come out of that consolidated permit and flow those through the application. But then we expect kind of that the turnaround time has been historically about 30 to 60 days in this instance.

Conclusion

I have now downgraded my rating for B2Gold from a Strong Buy to a Buy. My main concern is a classical sell on the news drop following the release of the Gramalote feasibility study. I believe there is an irrational rally in this stock as gold's spot price and the S&P500 are relatively flat, while the overall macro situation is cooling off.

From a volume perspective, this stock was the most active last Monday. To me, that is a positive sign, as it's getting attention from the market.

From a valuation perspective, the company is still trading at an attractive EV/EBITDA ratio of 3.1, despite the recent rally. With a forward P/CF of just 4.8, I believe the stock is still attractive. That said, expect some volatility around the publication of the Gramalote feasibility study results.

data.title: HQGO: Unconvincing New Growth ETF
data.description:
HQGO strategy

Hartford US Quality Growth ETF (NASDAQ:HQGO) started investing operations on 12/05/2023 and tracks the Hartford US Quality Growth Index. The fund has a portfolio of 224 stocks, a 30-day SEC yield of 0.59% and an expense ratio of 0.34%.

As described in the prospectus by Hartford Funds, the underlying index is focused on growth stocks and “utilizes an optimization process to help maximize exposure to quality while avoiding negative exposure to value and momentum”. The methodology starts from the 1000 largest US companies by free float market capitalization. They are ranked using a composite score based on the gross profitability to assets ratio and five-year sales growth. Companies in the top 50% are considered quality growth companies. They are weighted based on free float market capitalization adjusted with constraints on sector weights, position size, concentration and diversification. The index is reconstituted semi-annually and the portfolio turnover rate in the most recent fiscal year was 34%. This article will use as a benchmark the S&P 500 Index, represented by SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY).

Portfolio

HQGO has a focus on mega-cap companies (about 53% of asset value) and information technology (39%). The fund also has significant exposure in consumer discretionary (14%), communication (12%) and healthcare (11%). Other sectors weigh no more than 6%. Compared to the benchmark, HQGO moderately overweights these top four sectors, while it downplays financials, industrials, real estate, and ignores utilities.

The portfolio is quite concentrated, but risks related to individual companies are moderate. Indeed, the top 10 issuers, listed in the next table, represent 39.4% of asset value, and each of the two largest positions (Nvidia and Microsoft) weighs 5.5%.

data.description:
Market Summary 1st Quarter 2025
MSCI World Index -1.79%
S&P 500 Index (SPX)(SP500) -4.27%
German DAX Index +11.32%
French CAC 40 Index +5.79%
Nikkei 225 Index -9.91%
Brent Crude Oil +0.13%$74.74 a barrel
Gold +19.02%$3,123.57 an ounce
US Dollar -4.85%vs.yen-4.14% vs. euro

Source: Bloomberg, WM/Reuters.

Market Overview

The first quarter ushered in a change of US leadership and with it a change in global equity market dynamics.

Though 2025 started well for risk assets broadly, sour sentiment took hold of US markets in mid-February as concerns mounted about the economic impacts of the Trump administration’s policy priorities. Meanwhile, non-US markets appeared to be reinvigorated by plans for greater fiscal spending in Germany and China. In contrast with recent US dominance, the MSCI EAFE Index outperformed the S&P 500 Index handily during the first quarter, 6.9% to -4.3%, while US exposure drove a 1.8% decline in the MSCI World Index. In another trend reversal, value outperformed growth across markets.1

Of course, circumstances changed dramatically in the early days of the second quarter, as we will discuss later in this report.

Sentiment Journey

Trump’s election victory in November 2024 initially fueled another leg higher in equities, as markets hoped the campaign trail promises of pro-growth deregulation and stimulative tax policy would bolster earnings for a range of US companies amid steady economic growth and cooling inflation. This optimism began to fade well before Trump took office in late January, however, and the unraveling of the so-called “Trump trade” was underway in earnest by mid-February as it became clear that the new administration’s policy timeline prioritized tariffs and program cuts over stimulative measures. It wasn’t long before domestic stock markets had given back all of their post-election day gains and then some.2

Non-US markets were mostly unfazed, however, buoyed by the prospect of increased fiscal spending. Germany’s new governing coalition spearheaded legislation to permanently exempt defense spending above 1% of GDP from its restrictive constitutional debt brake and created a €500 billion infrastructure fund, allowing the European country with the most fiscal space the flexibility to deploy it.3 Earlier, the annual meeting of China’s parliament emphasized the need to boost consumption in the face of external threats like the escalating trade war with the US.4 This comes on top of plans to increase fiscal support by 1 to 2% of GDP this year.

In the US, hard economic data released in the first quarter continued to show persistent economic growth alongside stubbornly above-target inflation in the US. However, soft indicators appeared to capture the early negative impacts of the new administration’s policy erraticism and aggressive cost cutting. Consumers, for one, have grown decidedly more cautious in the face of the prevailing uncertainty. Consumer confidence as measured by The Conference Board has declined for four consecutive months, and its expectations index—which captures consumers’ short-term outlook for income, business and labor-market conditions—fell to a 12-year low in March and sits well below the threshold that usually signals coming recession.5 A survey from the University of Michigan indicated similarly crumbling sentiment,6 while the New York Fed’s latest consumer data revealed notable pessimism across prospects for household finances, employment, loan delinquencies and credit access.7

Business attitudes have also darkened. On Main Street USA, the NFIB Small Business Optimism Index depicted waning confidence in the economy and high and rising uncertainty in what the future holds.8 Globally, business confidence in the year ahead approached post-Covid lows in March, with US manufacturing Purchasing Managers’ Index (PMI) slipping to below 50 indicating expectations of contracting activity.9 Perhaps most tellingly, tariffs were mentioned more than 800 times in investor events or conference calls during the first quarter, the highest rate in 15 years.10 With this level of concern evident even before the announcement of Trump’s tariff plan, it seems likely the topic will dominate earnings calls in the weeks ahead.

Mind the Gap

While the environment has changed dramatically in the early days of the second quarter—more on that later—it’s worth noting that the US economy entered the year with an ongoing positive output gap, as well as below-average unemployment and above-average wage growth. A positive output gap indicates the economy is operating above potential, and the resulting inflationary impulse typically invites a cooling response from policymakers. For example, the Federal Reserve’s rate hikes in 2022 and 2023 in response to persistently high inflation narrowed but did not close the output gap. With monetary policy settings currently pretty close to neutral, the persistence of the positive output gap likely can be attributed to expansionary fiscal policy and ongoing deficit spending.11

Persistent deficits have also helped support corporate earnings, in our view, and skewed risk perception in US markets. From World War II through the global financial crisis, the country’s primary fiscal position was more or less in balance through decades of offsetting deficits and surpluses. Since 2009, however, economic cycles have been characterized by deeper primary deficits at the bottom and shallower deficits at the top.12 The level of S&P 500 earnings has more or less kept pace with the mounting pile of debt that has resulted from persistent deficit spending for much of the twenty-first century.13

Which is to say that as government debt goes, so too goes the nominal drift in the economy and corporate participation in it. Assuming that current levels of debt accumulation are needed to support expectations of continued earnings growth, a reacceleration in payroll growth, wage growth and inflation are plausible—though underappreciated—byproducts.

Between a Rock and a Hard Place

While it’s painless to acknowledge the structural deterioration of the country’s fiscal profile, fixing it is not. Easy fiscal policy begets difficult choices: Do nothing to contain spending and increase the risk of inflation, or take action to curb it and increase the risk of recession.

Federal outlays as a percentage of GDP have been chronically high since the onset of Covid-19 and amounted to around 23% in fiscal 2024.14 Dividing federal spending into its three major components— discretionary, net interest and mandatory—can help illuminate the challenges facing policymakers seeking to address the issue.

Discretionary spending comprises nondefense and defense spending and is funded annually through the congressional appropriations process. Nondefense discretionary spending supports a wide range of programs, from education and public health to scientific research and tax collections. While the Trump administration has identified nondefense discretionary spending as the lowest-hanging and most politically expedient fruit to pick, at 3.3% of GDP, it’s a small harvest. So while the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) makes headlines by taking a chainsaw to these outlays, its ability to impact the deficit and debt is minimal. For example, while a 20% cut to the nondefense discretionary budget would be significant to the programs impacted, it would only amount to about 0.6% of GDP.

On the defense side, spending has shifted markedly lower since the end of the Cold War and in 2024 comprised just 2.9% of GDP, slightly above the all-time low of 2.7% in 1999. Given the shifting alliances amid an increasingly shaky geopolitical landscape, it’s hard to imagine this rate going lower. The Trump administration has urged its allies in the North Atlantic Treaty Organization to increase their defense spending in the face of new and emerging threats, and Secretary of State Rubio has signaled that the US would follow suit.15 Trump himself recently announced that he plans to request $1 trillion for defense in the fiscal 2026 budget, up from about $850 billion in 2025.16

Assuming a unilateral restructuring or voluntary default of US debt is off the table, net interest spending is untouchable. The cost of servicing government debt has climbed rapidly in recent years as sub-2% debt issued during the Covid-19 period matured and was replaced by debt at rates in excess of 4%; this trend is forecast to continue.17 Notably, 2024 represented the first year that net interest spending exceeded defense spending. “Ferguson’s Law” posits that any power that spends more on debt servicing than on defense faces a heightened risk of imperial decline. With the US in violation of this law, there is perhaps some inevitability to the country’s pullback from the global stage.18

With both discretionary and net interest spending unlikely sources of relief, meaningful progress on US debt likely will require entitlement reform and/or higher revenue. The bulk of government outlays— representing 14.1% of GDP in 2024—are directed toward programs like Social Security, Medicare and Medicaid, mandatory commitments whose parameters are enshrined into law. Mustering the necessary political will for changes to programs so broadly popular with voters seems like an insurmountable challenge. Generating additional revenue through higher taxes is also appears difficult to reach with Republicans controlling the White House and both chambers of Congress.

Enter tariffs. Charged to importers at the port of entry into the US and mostly passed along to end consumers, tariffs act as a sort of backdoor consumption tax on Americans. Yes, they generate revenue for the federal government, but at the likely cost of higher prices and slower economic activity.

Trump championed the benefits of tariffs throughout his presidential campaign, and he quickly—if with shifting degrees of conviction— slapped new levies on specific countries (notably, Canada, Mexico and China) and industries (steel and aluminum) upon taking office.

Designating April 2 as “Liberation Day,” Trump appeared in the White House’s Rose Garden to introduce a wide-ranging package of tariffs, imposing a baseline 10% charge on all imports globally and steeper rates—referred to as, but not actually, “reciprocal”—on countries deemed to be “bad actors.”19

More extreme than markets seemed to anticipate, the tariff announcement unleashed a rout across risk assets worldwide and a significant spike in volatility. Perhaps of greater concern was the selloff in Treasuries and the US dollar, which may suggest wavering conviction in these assets as reliable “safe havens” during periods of unrest. A week later, via social media and with decidedly less fanfare, Trump announced that while the 10% baseline tariff would continue, the “reciprocal” tariff would be paused for all countries except China, which is now subject to a tariff of 145%.20

Seeking Ballast amid the Chaos

We make a point of acknowledging our inability to forecast the future even in the most placid of times, but Trumps’s to and fro approach to trade policy—which thus far has included tariffs, counter-tariffs, counter-counter-tariffs, tariff pauses, etc.—has introduced profound instability to the global order.

Given the lack of both historical comparisons and clarity around many important variables, directionality may be the best one can hope for in a forecast. To that end, the global tariff framework as currently designed likely would raise prices and slow economic activity in the US while weighing on both prices and economic growth abroad. Further, tariffs and their indeterminate impact reflect another investment risk in a world rife with them, the aggregate impact of which suggests a greater likelihood of nonlinear market moves such as those we have seen in recent days.

But rather than making concentrated bets on the direction of markets, we continue to focus on investing in a diversified basket of individual assets that we believe have the potential to demonstrate resilience across multiple states of the world. In certain portfolios, this includes gold, whose resilience year-to-date underscores why we advocate for a strategic exposure to the metal as a potential hedge against adverse market outcomes. Gold also serves as a source of long-term deferred purchasing power, easily convertible to cash when market dislocations produce opportunities to invest in high-quality businesses at prices that have suddenly become much more attractive.

Portfolio Review

Global Fund A Shares (without sales charge*) posted a return of 6.92% in first quarter 2025. Developed Europe and North America were the leading contributors while developed Asia excluding Japan was flattish and Japan also lagged. Materials, consumer staples and financials were largest contributors among equity sectors, while information technology, communication services and real estate were the only detractors. The Global Fund outperformed the MSCI World Index in the period.

Leading contributors in the First Eagle Global Fund this quarter included gold bullion, Philip Morris International Inc. (PM), Alibaba Group Holding Ltd. (BABA, OTCPK:BABAF), Wheaton Precious Metals Corp (WPM) and BAE Systems plc (OTCPK:BAESF, OTCPK:BAESY).

Gold continued to serve its role as a potential hedge against adverse market conditions during the quarter, setting a series of new nominal highs as the dollar slumped and real interest rates fell. Sustained geopolitical unrest continued to underpin demand from central banks, as did the potential fallout from a shifting and uncertain global trade framework.

Philip Morris reported strong results during the quarter, propelled by both smoke-free and combustible products. With costs denominated in US dollars but approximately two-thirds of revenues sourced from non-US markets, we believe the company should be positioned to benefit from a weaker US dollar. Investors’ recent interest in companies that consistently generate cash may have provided an additional tailwind, along with the reduced emphasis on socially responsible investing. Facilitated by strong operations, management has consistently returned cash to shareholders through dividends and stock repurchases.

Chinese technology giant Alibaba reported strong earnings during the quarter, reflecting robust e-commerce and cloud operations. With the dominant cloud infrastructure business in China, Alibaba should be positioned to benefit from Chinese investment in generative artificial intelligence (AI). Notably, Apple (AAPL) has selected Alibaba to develop AI features for its phones in China. Alibaba continues to invest in growing its businesses and improving operating efficiencies, even as it returns cash to shareholders through dividends and stock repurchases.

Wheaton Precious Metals, a Canadian streaming and royalty company, reported record-breaking revenue for the most recent quarter, driven by production that exceeded the upper end of expectations thanks to stronger-than-expected output at the Salobo and Constancia mines in Peru. Wheaton also guided solid production growth for the next several years. Wheaton benefits from capable management, a strong balance sheet and steady production from high-quality, low-cost mines, and—as a streamer—it has the luxury of reinvesting its robust cash flows only as attractive opportunities emerge.

BAE Systems is the largest defense contractor in the UK. The company is well positioned to benefit from prospectively easier fiscal policies in Europe as countries seek to increase their defense spending in the face of uncertain military support from the US. BAE’s long-term government contracts generate recurring revenues and provide cyclical resilience.

The leading detractors in the quarter were Oracle Corporation (ORCL), Alphabet Inc. Class C (GOOG, GOOGL), Salesforce.com, Inc. (CRM), Alphabet Inc. Class A and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co., Ltd. Sponsored ADR (TSM, OTC:TSMWF).

Oracle is one of the world’s largest independent enterprise software companies. The company reported lower-than-expected results for its most recent quarter and provided lower-than-expected forward guidance. Oracle cited capacity constraints as a factor in its sales miss but noted that its cloud infrastructure business bookings have increased. Oracle said capital expenditures would be higher than previously expected as it expands its data-center capacity. We believe Oracle should be positioned to capture recurring and sustainable revenue streams by providing services that support the use and adoption of artificial intelligence.

Shares of Alphabet, the parent company of Google and YouTube, traded lower after it reported slower-than-expected sales growth in its cloud business, which provides services that range from basic data storage to artificial intelligence-powered analytical tools. The company attributed this slowdown to capacity constraints and increased its capital expenditure budget in order to build out capacity. We believe that Alphabet’s market position remains strong, and we like the company’s focus on returning cash to shareholders through dividends and stock buybacks.

Shares of enterprise software developer Salesforce traded down after the company reported lower-than-expected results for its most recent quarter and provided lower-than-expected forward guidance. This prompted concerns about its transformation from a service business to an artificial intelligence-powered enterprise business (its Agentforce platform). We continue to like Salesforce’s operational strength, market dominance and focus on balancing growth with improving profitability.

Shares of Alphabet, the parent company of Google and YouTube, traded lower after it reported slower-than-expected sales growth in its cloud business, which provides services that range from basic data storage to artificial intelligence-powered analytical tools. The company attributed this slowdown to capacity constraints and increased its capital expenditure budget in order to build out capacity. We believe that Alphabet’s market position remains strong, and we like the company’s focus on returning cash to shareholders through dividends and stock buybacks.

Taiwan Semiconductor is the world’s largest semiconductor foundry, and includes Nvidia (NVDA), Broadcom (AVGO), Intel (INTC), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) and Apple among its clients. As a primary manufacturer of advanced chips used in generative artificial intelligence, the company has seen continued strong sales in this category. However, its stock traded down due to concerns about the potential impact of tariffs on its business. The company has made significant efforts in recent years to diversify its manufacturing base geographically and recently announced plans to increase its manufacturing capacity in the US.

We appreciate your confidence and thank you for your support.
