data.title: Enbridge: "Quintuple Vortex" Exemplified
data.description:
In investing, some basic metrics for quality are generally known to be true. Investors know that low capex needs are typically a good thing, all else equal. They're aware that debt is usually bad, and that the companies that perform the best often have little or no debt at all. In fact it was Peter Lynch himself who said:

It's very hard to go bankrupt if you don't have any debt. - Peter Lynch

Further, investors typically understand that share buybacks at low prices are a pretty efficient way (among others) to return capital to long-term shareholders, while empire-building and growth-through-acquisition are typically looked down upon by those investors prioritizing alpha over everything else. And lastly, investors often comprehend that dividend growth should not exceed earnings growth, but that both growth metrics would ideally be high.

I want to highlight a company that is beloved on Seeking Alpha, and one which I've owned in the past - and yet a company which fails on the five metrics I'll outline below. The metrics can combine to create a value-destructive vortex I want to coin the "Quintuple Vortex". Though stock prices may not demonstrate it yet, the company stuck in that vortex today is Enbridge (NYSE:ENB).

Enbridge has been discussed on Seeking Alpha to no end, so I'm not going to review the bare basics that most readers understand. Instead I'll review Enbridge's standing regarding the five metrics which I'll discuss in this article:

  1. High Debt
  2. Slow Growth
  3. High Capex Needs
  4. High Dividend Commitments
  5. High Shareholder Dilution

I'll be using Canadian dollars for the remainder of the article.

High Debt

Enbridge's enterprise value is nearly twice its market capitalization.

  • Market Capitalization: $140B
  • Enterprise Value: $250B

As a refresher, enterprise value combines the market capitalization of a company and its net debt position. The metric is useful for anyone looking to acquire the company in question, because that owner would be responsible for the debt of the company. As shareholders, that owner is us, though we elect the directors and the c-suite to take responsibility for the debt and the rest of the company.

So we can think to ourselves, "What is the total cost of the company?" That figure is currently $250B. The next question would be "How long until we can recouple that $250B?" That should lead us to the earnings or free cashflow of the company, which I'll get to next.

But first, let's look at the long term trend in debt growth. In 2015, long-term debt was $40B, growing to $97B in the TTM. If you add in deferred tax liabilities of a whopping $20B and lease obligations of $1B, as well as other non-current liabilities of $13B, the company is sporting $150B in debts owed. So not only does the investor need to justify the market capitalization of $140B, but he/she needs to find lifetime earnings worth much more than $150B in liabilities in order to justify a return to shareholders by judgment day.

Now, some investors would turn to book value to as a hedge against the requirement for the investor to find such large earnings. I would counter that the static book value of Enbridge's assets are in terminal decline. Without added debt and shareholder dilation, a pipeline rusts, a natural gas facility falls into disrepair, wind turbines cease to work and solar panels become inefficient or obsolete. As such, book value should be taken with a large grain of salt - instead of counting on it as a valuation backstop (currently pegged by Enbridge at $61B with a tangible book value of only $20B), investors should weigh what level of capex needs are required (which will come from earnings, shareholder dilution, debt and reduced dividend growth) in order to maintain that book value.

Another consideration which investors need to be aware of with regards to debt are interest rates. The case for Enbridge rests in part on the ever unknowable long-term average interest rate on its debt. For example, 3% interest payments on its growing debt (maybe a 1-1.5% Bank of Canada overnight rate) could be quite manageable for Enbridge and if known for certain would represent a potentially compelling opportunity. Conversely, interest rates coming out at 6-7% for Enbridge (or a Bank of Canada overnight rate of 4-5%) over the next three decades would fully kill the investment thesis by requiring eventual interest payments so large that earnings from continuing operations would be close to zero.

Slow Growth

Enbridge is decidedly not a high growth company, but in fact, it's hardly a slow growth company either. On a per share basis, the company has grown EBIT from $2.4B (divided by 868M outstanding shares in 2015 equals $2.77/share) to $11.0B (divided by 2,180M shares in the TTM equals $5/share). This is rosier than the company's adjusted EPS figures, which grew 2.7% annually over the last ten years. The difference is from interest payments (I) and taxes (T).

Now, a doubling of EBIT over 10 years is not terrible, especially with a large dividend along the way. However, the adjusted EPS figures are the more accurate account of Enbridge's earnings history, and it's the debt growth required to achieve this that is concerning. In 2015, total liabilities (which includes long-term debt, pension and other post-retirement benefits, deferred tax liabilities, capital leases and other non-current liabilities) totaled $62.2B. By the last twelve months, that figure as stated previously is $150B. So while EBIT per share grew 1.8x over ten years ($5/$2.77), the company's liabilities grew 2.4x over the same period.

High Capex Needs

As stated in the introduction, a company with high capex needs is always going to be a worry for investors. Indeed, it was cited by Charlie Munger that a constant reinvestment of earnings at Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) just in order to produce the next chip technology manufacturing processes was a important factor in their decision in selling the company in early 2023 just a few quarters after buying it in late 2022.

To further illustrate this point, I'll use BCE (BCE) as an example.

It's hard to fathom the damage done by more than a decade of ZIRP (Zero Interest Rate Policy), in not just creating zombie debt companies, but in the destruction of shareholder value when dynamics finally changed. This has been seen across several sectors known for high leverage/high capex requirements, such as the telecom industry and the REIT space.

Bell Canada's growing debt, and its ever growing capex requirements, have not only destroyed earnings growth in the last and next few years, but the result is a destruction in valuation which compounds negatively with falling earnings, a cut dividend, and a near 0% return for 15 years. The fact that investors were happy to value the company at around 16x EV/EBIT or 17x earnings over the last 12 years is hard to fathom given the known high capex and growing debt the company exhibited as a practice, but it's now clear where the true value of the company lies following the dividend cut and the earnings declines - both results of capex and debt-fueled and unsustainable expansion in the 2010s. Current higher long-term interest rate forecasts have further hurt valuations.

In Enbridge's case, the demand for energy and power should remain somewhat higher than the demand for internet and mobile data usage long-term, given the first will grow with AI and/or the economy, while the second will grow with population growth, historically a much slower rate. This results in energy and power tailwinds Enbridge has and will continue to profit from.

But the capex requirements to maintain and expand such infrastructure remain. As an example, the capex Enbridge has spent in the TTM was $7.25B, and this figure is simply spent to maintain Enbridge's massive infrastructure. Now, I'm sure some readers will suggest some of this was growth capex. I invite them to witness the below "return" from these endeavors over the past ten years.

Therefore, if any of the capex is growth capex it clearly should have been used to pay off debt or slow shareholder dilution over the past decade, because the above average returns on invested capital should not be satisfactory to any equities investor.

Let's recall what was mentioned in an earlier section - that operating income was $11B in the TTM - indicating that 66% or 2/3 of the company's EBIT is spent maintaining the machine that produces that EBIT. Also not that, after interest expenses of $4.85B in the TTM, the company is negative on EBT (Earnings before Taxes) after factoring in maintenance capex. Of course, the company's adjusted figures wouldn't demonstrate this.

My question to readers is as follows: if the company post-capex, post taxes of $2B in the TTM) is losing $3.1B in the TTM, where do you think your dividend is coming from? Even if you account for depreciation and amortization of $5.4B, the remaining "real" earnings post-capex, post-taxes, post-interest payments, are far lower than the adjusted EPS would indicate. Remember that the dividend payment is $8B, indicating debt growth and shareholder dilution are critical for sustained dividends, let alone dividend increases.

High Dividend Commitments

I want to circle back to the beginning general thesis regarding the "Quadruple Vortex". The reason why high debt, high capex requirements, shareholder dilution, slow earnings growth and high dividend commitments are such a powerful negative vortex is partly because of precedence. Many of those items are not negotiable, or extremely unpopular.

In the case of dividends, Enbridge maintains a record of over 30 years of dividend increases, which has provided income investors with that level of stability for a generation. The dividend payment is nearly $8B in the TTM (against $11B in EBIT, before the $4.85B in interest payments, $7.25B in capex and $2B in taxes). Cutting the dividend is going to face huge investor backlash, and as such is likely something management has not fully thought through.

Investors will point to the company's term "Distributable Cashflow", a very rosy metric which helps demonstrate that the dividend payout ratio is in the 60% range. This sets investors at ease, despite the basic dynamics described above, where there is so much capex and interest payments that the company has long turned to building debt and diluting shareholders.

High Shareholder Dilution

Over the last ten years (from 2015 to the TTM), Enbridge has diluted shareholders 151%. That means if the stock was $50 in 2015 and no other dynamics occurred, the stock would now be $20. Of course, Enbridge was growing assets and book value through this period, as well as EBIT and other metrics.

Much of the shareholder dilution came in 2017 with the purchase of Spectra Energy, but a further ~10% of dilution has happened since then, with most years demonstrating the same trend.

As discussed above, the company is less profitable than we all want to admit, and the dividend is not covered under any reasonable analysis of the income statement or cashflow statement. As such, if the dividend is expected to me maintained, investors should expect further shareholder dilution and debt accumulation.

This has so far worked, in a similar way to Bell Canada's debt build-up and shareholder dilution policy worked, for decades - until it stopped working. The company reached its "saturation point" - the point at which the company can no longer expect to grow its debt, achieve a proper return on invested capital, dilute shareholders, increase the dividend, maintain its capital expenditures, pay its interest, AND maintain investor confidence (aka, valuations). Refer to the earlier chart of BCE's earnings and EPS growth in recent years, and the resulting valuation plunge.

I believe this level of readjustment will not be as dramatic for Enbridge. But I believe a readjustment in the expectation of real earnings to the company's owners (again, post-interest payments, post-dilution, post-taxes, post-capex, etc.) will eventually sort itself out and that investors will have no choice but to value the company more conservatively when the buck stops.

Valuation

Somewhere near the beginning of this article, I asked readers the question:

"What is the total cost of the company?" That figure is currently $250B. The next question would be "How long until we can recouple that $250B?"

I want to quote my own writing here to help this question stand out to skimmers of the article. This is fundamental. If it takes more than 10 years to recoup that $250B, very few investors should likely become disinterested, given that return, all else equal, would be 10% CAGR. But I'm going to run through some math to get a ballpark estimate on the number of years it will take to recoup our investment.

As stated before, EBIT in the TTM was $11.02B. I'm going to subtract $4.85B in interest payments, and $2B in taxes. To extend an olive branch to Enbridge bulls, I'm going to assume no further debt accumulation or shareholder dilution, and I'm going to add back in depreciation and amortization. I'm going to subtract half of capex, or $3.63B, assuming the remaining growth capex is productive, which it hasn't been historically.

$11.02B - $4.85B - $2B + 5.38B - $3.63B = $5.92B. But because I'm prudent I'm going to assume higher rates over the next decade, so instead of an interest payment of $4.85B in the TTM, I'm going to assume an annual interest payment of $6B, therefore subtracting a further $1.15 from the total.

This leaves owners with $4.77B in what I'll call something close to "owner's earnings", annually. And I'll assume generously that this grows at 3% (remember, without further debt accumulation over the next ten years). Because that is about what I expect inflation to be over the next decade, we'll assume $4.77 x 10 = aggregate owner's earnings over the next decade, which comes to $47.B. This is a long, long way off $250B, and no longer worth calculating how many years it would take to recoup the rest of our investment.

A totally different way to value Enbridge is historically. As such, it still doesn't make sense, trading at a blended adjusted earnings (the company's own rosy figures) of 22.6x.

A Way Forward

Before I conclude, I want to showcase what it would take from management for me to be interested in the shares anywhere near these levels.

As shown in the return on invested capital graph above, management has not been good at creating an excellent return for shareholders. Recall that Mark Zuckerberg bought Instagram for $1B in 2012, and it's easily worth $70B now. In 2017, Enbridge bought Spectra for $37B, and it's not wildly obvious that those assets are worth much more than that eight years later.

Given the lack of a history of return on invested capital and the fact that Enbridge's debt is so large, an effective way to generate return for shareholders (and help justify current valuations) would be to pay down debt, given that in the TTM, interest costs were almost exactly 5%. This is larger than Enbridge's return on invested capital, and larger than its earnings growth over the last ten years. Further, despite euphoric EPS growth estimates over the next two years, EPS growth will likely be lower than 5% as an average over the next ten years, justifying debt repayment.

If management were to pay down debt by half, and thereby alleviate interest payments by half, the company would be de-levered to a point where buying in the $50 range (roughly $36 USD) would start to look attractive. Without this commitment, I believe investors should demand a far more de-risked valuation closer to $40 (roughly $29 USD) at the bare minimum for a satisfactory long-term investment, where judgement day does arrive on its debt, capex and dilution practices, as we've seen with BCE.

Conclusion

One could view Enbridge's value proposition as hinging on valuation. At lower valuations, as were seen in the Fall of 2020 and Fall of 2023, such valuations can effectively be seen as a de-risking of the company's debt, remembering that whether it's market capitalization or net debt, it's the total enterprise value for which the buying shareholder is responsible.

On the other extreme, valuations near where they are currently, as well as mid-2022 and the entire 2011-2019 period, contribute to underperformance risks which materialized historically. In the face of growing debt, uncertain interest rates, shareholder dilution, unattractive valuations and other factors mentioned in this article, it would not be prudent buying here.

As such, for income investors reliant on dividends who seek to open a position in Enbridge, I recommend waiting for such valuations to return, and until then, seeking alpha and/or dividends elsewhere where valuations are extreme in the other direction (Stellantis (STLA), Alexandria Real Estate (ARE) and Pfizer (PFE) come to mind). For investors already holding Enbridge shares, the decision is more difficult as you're potentially income dependent - but the forward outperformance is equally unlikely.

This is not a case of "trimming the flowers and watering the weeds", because there is no conceivable way Enbridge is a flower. As such, I'm rating Enbridge a Sell, indicating a strong chance of market underperformance into the long-term, but shying away from a Strong Sell, indicating permanent immanent capital depreciation as seen in the BCE example.

data.title: Norfolk Southern: Get On Board This Dividend Stock Now
data.description:
Where markets go tomorrow, next week, next month, or next year, I won't waste anyone's time with a guess. I don't have a crystal ball.

Instead, I prefer to focus on company fundamentals. That's because, over the long haul, share price performance largely tracks with EPS growth. This is why I aim to buy/cover businesses with positive growth outlooks trading at what I believe to be at or below fair value.

Norfolk Southern (NYSE:NSC) is a company that I believe has an encouraging future ahead. When I last covered the stock with a buy rating in February, I liked its combination of modest revenue growth forecast for 2025, efficiency gains, and a resumption of share repurchases. I also appreciated NSC's BBB+ credit rating from S&P on a stable outlook. My fair value estimate was also above the share price at the time.

Today, I'm standing by my buy rating. NSC's industrial development pipeline is currently at a historic high. The company continues to become more efficient. NSC is also financially stable. Finally, the railroad operator is slightly undervalued.

The Robust Industrial Development Pipeline Has Me Cautiously Optimistic

On April 23rd, NSC shared its first-quarter earnings report for the period ended March 31st, 2025. The company's total operating revenue decreased 0.4% over the year-ago period to $2.99 billion in the quarter. For more color, this came in at $30 million above Seeking Alpha's analyst consensus during the quarter.

Overall, I thought the company's results were respectable. Why do I believe that a flat top line is a decent result?

Well, it has to do with NSC's fuel surcharge revenue. Unsurprisingly, this revenue source is linked to fuel prices. Lower fuel prices relative to Q1 2024 led NSC's fuel surcharge revenue to fall by 22.3% year-over-year to $202 million for the first quarter. Backing this out, railway operating revenue less fuel surcharge revenue edged 1.7% higher over the year-ago period to $2.79 billion in the quarter.

Merchandise category revenue (less fuel) grew by 2% year-over-year to $1.80 billion during the first quarter. According to opening remarks from Chief Commercial Officer Ed Elkins during the Q1 2025 Earnings Call, volume was down by 2% for the quarter. Gains in the Chemicals and Ag businesses were counteracted by weakness in the Metals and Construction segments. A 4% increase in revenue per unit to a new quarterly record was the major contributing factor to the category's overall topline growth.

NSC's Intermodal category revenue (less fuel) increased by 5% over the year-ago period to $629 million in the first quarter. That was driven by a 3% year-over-year increase in volume, which was the result of gains in both domestic and international per Elkins. Thanks to stabilization in truck pricing, revenue per unit also grew by 2% over the year-ago period.

NSC's Coal category revenue (less fuel) declined by 5% year-over-year to $366 million for the first quarter. Reduced coal export prices were only partially offset by volume gains in its utility business. According to Elkins, this was powered by strong electricity demand and support from higher natural gas prices.

Moving to the bottom line, NSC's adjusted diluted EPS surged 8% higher over the year-ago period to $2.69 during the first quarter. This was $0.01 better than the Seeking Alpha analyst consensus in the quarter. That was fueled by a 200-basis-point improvement in the adjusted operating ratio to 67.9% for the quarter. This is how adjusted diluted EPS grew during the quarter as total operating revenue was down slightly.

Looking ahead, NSC has several tailwinds going for it. For one, the company's industrial development pipeline closed out Q1 2025 at a historic high.

There were 517 projects in the pipeline and activity was up 25% relative to Q1 2024. This is because there's strong interest from companies to expand domestic production, as well as international companies seeking to locate new manufacturing facilities in the United States. Elkins highlighted sectors like steel manufacturing, construction materials, and food production as seeing an increase in project activity in the latter half of the first quarter. Additionally, project activity momentum built through April.

Secular trends like near-shoring and reshoring played a role. As did NSC's reputation for helping customers to reduce their costs in their supply chains.

The company's 116 most recently announced projects are expected to add 150,000 new annualized carloads in volume. This is set to fully ramp up over three years.

Another tailwind for NSC is its ongoing PSR 2.0 Transformation program. In 2024, the company generated $292 million in cost savings and productivity gains. For 2025, the company is on track to generate another $150 million in cumulative cost savings and productivity gains. Incremental savings and productivity gains are also expected for 2026.

The final piece of the growth puzzle for NSC is the resumption of its share repurchase program. In Q1 2025, the company repurchased nearly $250 million of shares. As incremental share repurchases continue, that should help the share count gradually go lower over time.

For these reasons, I'm inclined to agree with the FAST Graphs analyst consensus. For 2025, it's predicted that NSC's diluted EPS will grow by 7.1% to $12.69. Another 12.6% rise in diluted EPS to $14.29 is the current consensus in 2026. For 2027, an additional 12.7% jump in diluted EPS to $16.10 is being forecasted.

From a balance sheet perspective, NSC is also doing well. The company's debt-to-capital ratio in the low-50% range per FAST Graphs supports a BBB+ credit rating from S&P on a stable outlook (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all details in this subhead were according to NSC's Q1 2025 Earnings Press Release and NSC's Q1 2025 Earnings Presentation).

Shares Remain Undervalued

Over the last three months, NSC's shares have generated a 1% total return. That's the mirror opposite of the 1% decline in the S&P 500 index (SP500) over that time. Overall, I believe NSC's shares remain modestly discounted.

The railroad operator's shares are priced at a current-year P/E ratio of 19.1 and a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 18.2. For more perspective, this is less than the 10-year average P/E ratio of 19.6, according to data from FAST Graphs.

I continue to think that the 10-year average P/E ratio is a realistic fair value assumption in the years to come. This is because the aforementioned strength of NSC's industrial development pipeline suggests the growth story is holding up. The annual forward diluted EPS growth consensus of 11.3% over the next few years lines up with past growth rates (when accounting for cyclical peaks and troughs).

The calendar year 2025 is 42% complete. That means another 58% of 2025 and 42% of 2026 is yet to come over the next 12 months. This yields a forward 12-month diluted EPS input of $13.36.

Using my fair value P/E ratio of 19.6, I compute a fair value of $261 a share. Compared to the current $243 share price (as of June 2nd, 2025), this works out to a 7% discount to fair value. If NSC lives up to growth expectations and returns to fair value, it could generate a 33% cumulative upside through 2027.

The Dividend Should Begin Picking Up Steam

NSC's 2.2% forward dividend yield clocks in meaningfully higher than the industrial sector's median forward dividend yield of 1.6%. This is enough for Seeking Alpha's Quant System to issue B+ and B- grades for forward dividend yield and overall dividend yield, respectively.

Recently, NSC's dividend growth has stalled out. Following an 8.9% raise in the quarterly dividend per share to $1.35 in 2023, the dividend was kept in line in 2024. Even with this being the case, the five-year annual dividend growth rate was still solid at 7.5%. This is comparable to the sector median of 7.6%. That's sufficient for a B- grade for the metric and an A grade for overall dividend growth from the Quant System.

There's every reason to believe that NSC will resume dividend growth in the next six to 12 months. That's because, aside from the return to stronger growth, the company's dividend is also well-covered by profits. NSC's diluted EPS payout ratio is expected to register in the low-40% range for 2025. Per The Dividend Kings' Zen Research Terminal, this would be comfortably below the 60% payout ratio that rating agencies like to see from the industry. The free cash flow payout ratio is also projected to come in at around 52% in 2025. That's why the Quant System awards an A grade to NSC for overall dividend safety.

This puts the company in a position to begin a new dividend growth streak and build on many decades of uninterrupted dividend payments (including its predecessor, Norfolk & Western Railway). That warrants a C+ grade from the Quant System for overall dividend consistency.

Risks To Consider

NSC is a fundamentally solid business. However, there are still risks that could impact the investment thesis. As a result, I'll be going over a few risks that I believe should be monitored over time.

Similar to my last article, NSC's results tend to be correlated with the U.S. economy. The U.S. recession betting odds on Kalshi for 2025 have come down from almost 70% in April to just 30% today. If that begins to trend in the wrong direction again and a recession does manifest, NSC's growth could significantly slow or invert to negative growth for a year. This could also hit the stock's sentiment and lead to a selloff.

Another macro risk to NSC is the possibility that secular trends like near-shoring and re-shoring could eventually run out of momentum. Chief Commercial Officer Ed Elkins emphasized that the company is noting cases where decision timelines are being extended as customers evaluate the macroeconomic environment. That could lead to a slowdown in industrial development activity, which could have at least some impact on its growth prospects.

Another key risk to NSC is the potential for a widespread cyber breach. Being a company that's highly important to the logistics of the United States, it does take cyber security very seriously. However, hackers only have to be right one time to inflict major damage to the company's growth profile via compromised customer data and resulting lawsuits.

One last operating risk to NSC is that it transports hazardous materials on its rail network. Another derailment on the scale of East Palestine, Ohio, could cost the company billions of dollars.

Summary: Norfolk Southern Is A Buyable Dividend Stock

NSC's fundamental outlook is promising. Industrial development activity was up significantly over the past year. NSC is also executing its cost-savings program. The company's balance sheet is investment-grade with a stable outlook.

Yet, shares are just as undervalued as they were three months ago. That's why I believe low-double-digit annual total returns are likely for the foreseeable future. Consequently, I'm reaffirming my buy rating.

data.title: AGGH: Investment Grade Exposure With Credit Hedging
data.description:
Introduction

The Simplify Aggregate Bond ETF (NYSEARCA:AGGH) emerges as one of the most innovative solutions in recent times within the fixed income universe. Launched in February 2022 by Simplify, the vehicle not only passively replicates the aggregate investment grade bond market (through its position in the iShares Core U.S. Aggregate Bond ETF, AGG), but also incorporates an active credit hedging overlay.

Approximately 97% of its assets are used to replicate the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index through the iShares Core U.S. Aggregate Bond ETF (AGG), giving it access to US Treasuries, government agencies, investment grade corporate bonds and mortgage-backed securities.

The remaining 3% is used in short-term liquid instruments, mainly T-Bills and short credit derivative positions (e.g. selling CDS protection on IG corporate indices) to mitigate losses when credit spreads widen. Thus, if investors demand a higher premium in a deteriorating economic environment, the ETF offsets part of the fall in the price of the corporate bond portion within AGG through the gains of its credit hedge.

Conversely, when spreads compress, the cost of maintaining that hedge is limited and the fund captures much of AGG's appreciation. In short, the investment vehicle's managers seek to offer an attractive total return, while reducing drawdowns in episodes of credit stress, while retaining the investment grade fixed income exposure that characterizes the US aggregate bond market.

Fund Performance

In the image below, we can see the historical comparison of the vehicle in question, relative to its benchmark (Bloomberg US Aggregate) and some peer funds. As can be seen, the returns of AGGH (purple line) and the Bloomberg US Aggregate index (orange line) are very similar over the five years, although AGGH shows a slight overperformance, especially at credit market peaks, thanks to its hedging overlay. The iShares Government Bond ETF (blue line) also tracks the index closely, performing almost identically to the traditional AGG, while the Simplify Short Term Treasury Futures Strategy ETF (green line) consistently underperforms, reflecting its focus on ultra-short duration. All in all, AGGH manages to stay in line with the aggregate US bond, capturing almost all of its appreciation net of fees and offering a little extra thanks to its credit hedging strategy.

Analysis - Macro Fundamental

We will now analyze various indicators to study the current situation. Then, based on this analysis and looking at the future outlook, we will make a recommendation to buy/sell or hold the vehicle under analysis.

In the following graphs, we show the evolution of credit spreads (OAS) for the ICE BofA US Corporate Index and its BB sub-index. As we can see, IG spreads (both aggregate and BB tranche) remain at more or less constant levels, indicating that the market is not yet discounting a substantial deterioration in the credit quality of investment grade issuers. At the same time, High Yield spreads occasionally break upwards, but return to moderate ranges, a sign that risk appetite in the more speculative segments remains in place.

In this context, AGGH benefits from moderate credit compression. When investment grade OAS tightens, the underlying index - AGG - appreciates and the hedging overlay held by the investment vehicle under review incurs relatively low hedging costs, capturing almost all of the appreciation, net of fees. On the other hand, if spreads were to widen in the BBB tranche, which represents a large part of the investment grade universe, the vehicle would mitigate much of the decline because its short CDS position would generate gains that offset the loss in corporate bond prices. The current performance of these three spreads suggests that, as long as OAS remain at their levels or tend to compress, AGGH can continue to outperform a pure investment grade passive fund.

The chart below shows the implied probabilities in the Fed funds futures market for the FOMC meeting on 10 December 2025. As we can see, the highest probability (39.3%) points to rates being between 375 bps and 400 bps by then, while there is a significant probability (26.0%) that they will be at 400-425 bps and another (22.0%) that they will fall to 350-375 bps.

Concretely, the market discounts that the Fed will keep fed funds in a roughly stable range (425-450 bps) with little chance of drastic cuts (only 0.1% of them going down to 100-125 bp) or aggressive hikes.

Summary and Conclusion

Combining both analyses, we arrive at the following conclusions for the AGGH ETF.

If the markets believe that the Fed will not cut rates significantly until December 2025, the short-term yield curve will remain relatively flat, making it difficult for IG spreads to compress excessively. In this neutral scenario, investment grade OAS would tend to remain stable or compress slightly, benefiting the ETF because it could capture the appreciation of its underlying asset at a low hedging cost.

Secondly, if the odds shift towards a more aggressive cut (350–375 bps in December), this could imply a possible flattening of the curve and less pressure on short rates. At the same time, this signal could drive spread compression (especially the BBB spread) and generate a rally in the AGG price. Here too, the ETF would be in a position to maximize net gains, as the overlay would barely increase the credit carry.

Thirdly, if the markets were to discount that the Fed will maintain high rates (400–425 bps or more) due to persistent inflationary pressures, we could see a moderate widening of credit spreads, as prolonged tightening cools economic activity and increases the risk of corporate defaults. In this context, the vehicle's overlay would play its defensive role, limiting the drawdown of the underlying AGG in the event of a sudden widening of spreads, especially if the BBB tranche moves away from 1.10% towards higher levels.

In conclusion, my recommendation is to hold on to the vehicle if you have it in your portfolio. I do not recommend buying this fund at the moment, due to the volatility in the fixed income markets and the uncertainty surrounding the Trump administration's tariff war.

data.title: Landmark Bancorp: Growing Its Footprint In The Sunflower State
data.description:
I have reviewed a number of community banks recently, and several of the smallest ones have been the top performers over the last year from a stock price perspective. That is definitely the case with Landmark Bancorp (NASDAQ: LARK), a company whose stock has appreciated over 55% since June 2, 2024. After seeing these above-average returns, I felt it was worth taking a deeper dive to see if the stock represents a good value moving forward.

Based in Manhattan, Kansas, the small regional bank has grown organically for over a decade. The last time the bank made an acquisition of any kind was 2013. I tend to prefer regional banks that take a more measured approach to their asset growth internally, as buyouts often lead to added costs and increased share counts in the short term.

In spite of Landmark’s prudent acquisition strategy, there does not appear to be enough good news in its latest financial reports to give the stock a buy rating based on its current valuation. I think that shareholders who were able to benefit from the stock’s rise in 2024 should not expect the same performance this year for reasons that I will explain here.

Company Overview

Landmark Bancorp is the holding company for Landmark Bank, a community bank with 30 locations in 24 communities across the Sunflower State. LARK is actually incorporated in the state of Delaware, even though all of its bank branches are in Kansas, so that the company can benefit from the favorable governance regulations and lower administrative costs that come with Delaware corporate law. The bank’s origins date back to 1885 when it was founded under the name Manhattan Buildings & Savings Association. Based on my research, LARK is now the only publicly traded bank that operates entirely within the state borders of Kansas.

At the end of Q1 2025, Lankmark Bancorp had a market cap of $168.3 million. The bank had just over $1.58 billion in total assets, with $1.34 billion in deposits and a loan portfolio valued at $1.07 billion. The majority of the bank’s loans are allocated toward commercial real estate and one-to-four family residential real estate. Both categories comprise about a third of the portfolio. The balance is primarily commercial loans (17.8%) and agricultural loans (9.5%). The consolidated balance sheet for the bank, including the breakdown of the loan diversification as of March 31, 2025, is shown below.

The stock currently pays a quarterly dividend of $0.21, giving LARK a yield of 2.89% at its current price. The company has also paid out stock dividends of 5% at the end of the year for 24 consecutive years. While there are certainly advantages to paying non-cash dividends, it is not my preferred way for a company to reward shareholders. When one thinks about the impact of what amounts to a 21-20 share stock split for 24 years, the dilution of ownership and earnings is significant. However, Landmark has also authorized share repurchases as recently as 2020. The process of issuing share buybacks while also offering stock dividends seems like a cycle of unneeded expenses for LARK and its shareholders.

Analysis of Landmark Bancorp

Landmark Bancorp has been operating outside of the spotlight over the last several years. The stock has not been profiled on Seeking Alpha since 2019, and there are currently no analysts who cover the stock. This was affirmed when there were no questions asked at the end of the company’s most recent quarterly conference call. While I often see a lack of coverage as a positive, as it increases the chances of finding a “diamond in the rough”, it also makes research more challenging as there are no real accepted expectations for the year ahead.

The company’s most recent annual report paints a solid picture of growing earnings and book value over the last three years. Earnings per share increased 6.1% in 2024 after a 24.6% increase in 2023. Book value rose by almost 7% last year after a 13.8% gain the year prior. Return on average assets rose three basis points to 0.83% in 2024 while return on average equity retreated from 10.7% to 10.0% during that same period.

Fast forward to the most recent financial report from Q1 2025, and there is more good news to be found. LARK reported GAAP EPS of $0.81 and revenues of $16.5 million over the three month period. The EPS number was a 42% improvement from Q4 2024 and 68.8% higher than the same quarter last year. Return on average assets, return on average equity, and net interest margin also improved significantly, as shown in the snapshot below. The bank’s net interest margin is now above the national average, according to the FDIC.

Even with all of these positive results, however, Landmark is currently trading at its highest valuation in PE, P/ Cash Flow, and Price/ Book since before the pandemic. Its Price/ Earnings Growth ratio is 2.16. Many of the other small regional banks that I have profiled recently trade at a PEG ratio near or just below 1.

There are a few other minor concerns that I have with a recent decline in non-interest income that coincided with an increase in non-interest expenses. The higher expenses were attributed to an increase in bank staff and higher employee benefit costs. Overall, I think most of the bank’s attributes are positive, but the recent dramatic rise in the price of the stock has caused its valuation to become just a bit stretched.

While I do not think this is as significant as my other findings, it is also worth noting that the balance of insider transactions this year has leaned heavily toward selling, as two directors have sold almost 33,000 common shares at a value of $816,500. These two sellers were Patrick Alexander, the current Chairman of the Board of Directors, and Michael Scheopner, Landmark’s former CEO. All of their transactions were made in the open market in February.

Peer Analysis for Landmark Bancorp

Landmark has a section on its Investor Relations website that compares various key financial metrics for the bank to a set of peers that includes Enterprise Financial Services Corp (EFSC), Great Southern Bancorp (GSBC), and Simmons First National Corporation (SFNC). All three of these banks are significantly larger than LARK and may be able to achieve certain economies of scale due to their larger size. Nonetheless, Landmark is below the peer average in ROA and NIM. It also had a higher efficiency ratio, meaning that the bank is spending more to generate a dollar of income. I would prefer this ratio for LARK to be much closer to, if not below, 60% for me to feel better about an investment here. To be fair, the ratio for the bank was almost 70% at the end of 2024, so at its current rate of 66.45%, this is another financial metric that is moving in the right direction.

Risks to Consider

I see two significant risks to consider before investing in Landmark Bancorp. The first is the bank’s significant exposure to commercial loans. Any overall contraction in the US economy caused by higher inflation, trade conflicts, or higher unemployment could be a negative for the bank and its customers. Higher interest rates could mean that the bank has to pay more interest to attract new deposits or face tightening margins in the year ahead.

The second risk would be the bank’s limited footprint in Kansas, which makes its operations more sensitive to regional employment trends and political instability. It is worth noting that the outlook for the state economy has been gradually trending higher over the last four years. The annual Rich States, Poor States survey conducted by the American Legislative Exchange Council has Kansas ranked 23rd in 2025. This marks the fourth straight year of improvement since the Sunflower State ranked 39th in 2021. Nonetheless, Kansas’s economy is especially sensitive to the overall health of the American farming industry. While the remainder of 2025 is projected to be healthy, Kansas State University recently predicted a 24% decline in farm income for 2026. While LARK is not completely exposed to this sector of the economy with only 10% of its loans in this space, lower income for farmers could also mean fewer deposits and a decline in loan growth moving forward.

Conclusion

There is no doubt that Landmark’s price appreciation over the last year is impressive. However, there is nothing I see in my research that leads me to believe that a performance like that can be repeated in the next 12 months.

I do think the stock is worth watching if there is a meaningful correction in its price over the next quarter. However, at the current time, I rate the stock as a hold. Investors who may be looking for other small regional banks for possible investments may want to read through my recent analysis of Chicago's Byline Bancorp (BY) or Louisiana-based Home Bancorp (HBCP).

data.title: Pentair: After Strong RoR Over 2 Years, What Comes Next? (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Dear readers/followers,

Sometimes you make a great investment, and don't make it significant enough. You invest, but then the company goes up, and you believe the company has gone "too far," so you don't really invest more, only to see the company go further and further. The position is too small to act decisively on, but it's not big enough to sell for a significant return or profit - so you kind of keep it to see where it ends up going.

That description is a good way to characterize what happened to my position in Pentair (NYSE:PNR).

This was an investment that I wrote about towards the tail-end of 2022 in an article you can find here. You can see in that article that at the time of publication, the company was in a post-COVID-19 dip, and I bought stock at $44/share. Today, the company is almost at triple digits. This marks a dividend-inclusive RoR that looks as follows.

As you can see, a very successful investment - and I still have my shares/position of below 0.3% left. I wish I had invested 10 times as much - but that's sometimes how it goes. Today, I will show you what I intend to do with this position and how I intend to move forward with Pentair.

Pentair - Following up on a great business with excellent returns

When I covered Pentair, I believe it was indisputably undervalued. This company has a moat, it has excellent fundamentals. Pentair, for those unfamiliar with the company, is a pure-play water company, working with sustainable water solutions. You know, if you follow my work, how much I like investing in water or water-adjacent companies or technology companies. Pentair is no different.

The company's focus is on pumps and filtration - and as you can imagine, few things could be more important when it comes to either industrial or consumer applications for water. The company, now 2-3 years after my last article, is still organized in 3 main segments consisting of Pool, Water Solutions, and Flow. Pool is perhaps the somewhat more luxury-focused and volatile of the bunch, with headwinds faced during the decline in 2023 both due to macro, but also a significant inventory correction. However, as of the latest few months and the latest quarterly results, Pool has seen some recovery.

The company has an excellent business model because, aside from developing new products, Pentair obviously services a vast existing asset base of filtration and pumping assets across the world, and over 75% of the current products it sells are not new products but replacement products to existing customers.

In 2022, slightly before my article, the company also acquired Manitowoc Ice, which saw Pentair enter the commercial ice/cubed ice market. I continue to view this as an excellent play into the commercial food and food service industry.

My baseline thesis in my last article was a mid-single-digit EPS CAGR, and the company has delivered this. What I didn't necessarily expect was the increased premiumization we have been seeing, where Pentair now trades in excess of 21x P/E, which we'll go into in the valuation section.

If you look at the company from a high level, you can expect a pure-play water business like this to see increased and sustained demand from efficient water solutions and sustainability tech. And indeed, I would see these as the main advantages. The company's pool business is back to growth both on the top line and bottom line, with market share gains and good operating margins is also a positive. Also, Pentair has been a sort of "how-to" in driving efficiency through internal transformation. The improvements Pentair has been able to its EBIT/Operating margin have been impressive, simply from reshuffling and reorganizing things.

Furthermore, the company is poised to become a dividend king in the next year. It generates over $4B of annual sales on an LTM basis, and while it's incredibly US-focused, that just means to me that there's potential for expansion in the future.

Also, as you can see above, the company has one of the lowest leverage in this entire sector. The one downside is that the company's yield is beyond just "modest". I would, at 1.02%, characterize it as "low". However, when you look at the company's multi-year performance, even including COVID-19, it's very hard to fault or be negative about the company here.

As I said early on, I wish I had invested 10x the amount I did - but at the time, there were many opportunities on the market, and I had a somewhat more diversified investment approach than I have today. Seeing a dividend aristocrat at the valuation we saw back then, I would likely have invested at least 4-5x what I did. It is, I believe, when it comes to investing (and in life), about doing "better next time."

The company's Q1'25 results continue to show impressive margin expansion despite a changing macro landscape. Given the company's operations, tariff impacts are not negligible, but also not massive. The company's 2025 guidance already included tariffs as early as February, and now, as of Q1'25, also includes the communicated tariff plans for April. For the full year, Pentair expects top-line growth of 2%, with EBIT up around 6-9% and EPS up 7-11% due to further increased savings. The low dividend also gives the company ample room to execute both on continued low leverage and internal efficiencies, as well as the potential for inorganic growth.

What I also like about Pentair is the company's capital-light model, with a service focus. What I mean by capital-light is that 75% of the company's business is a two-step dealer/distribution model, with only around 25% OEM/projects. The geographical focus also works in favor of the company at this particular time, though I say again, the future might hold potential for further expansion for the company outside of the US.

Management seems very apt at what it's doing - I reach this conclusion based on over-delivery of its savings results as well as maintaining high sales forecasts and EPS guidance (and previously executed targets) for the 2025E target.

Risks for the company? Every company and investment has risks, but I will say that when it comes to Pentair, I believe the risks are mostly related to valuation, which I will show you below. Yes, growth in the past 5 years has been lower than growth in the past 20, and cost inflation is a thing. Much of the company's growth and margin improvements have been internally focused, and these are not repeatable feats, but still positive. We can see this with the 2% top-line growth trend, and at some point, the well of internal efficiencies will have dried up, meaning the company will be left with whatever organic and inorganic growth it can add to its numbers. Furthermore, the tariff and the current geopolitical situation are likely to make the company's non-US business a bit more challenging, and while tariff impacts are not massive, they are not small either ($140M or thereabout).

However, Pentair has a moat, Pentair has a superb and working business model.

So my question becomes, what can we say that the business model is worth in 2025E and looking forward to 2027E?

Valuation for Pentair - no longer attractive, I'm moving to "HOLD" and considering rotation

In my last article, I was unequivocally positive about Pentair - it was a 5/5 "BUY", and I followed my own stance, a position I still hold today.

However, the company is now up more than 100%, inclusive of dividends, and when looking at the valuation fundamentals, I cannot see a clear enough upside to continue to be positive here.

Let me show you what I mean.

Pentair currently trades at 21.8x normalized compared to a 5-year average of 20x, and a 20-year average of 14.6x P/E. That's a massive increase, and not one I am sure is justified by the future potential. As I noted, the internal efficiencies at some point do not represent repeatable feats, and at that point we move from 9-11% EPS growth to less than 3-4% EPS growth If 5-year top-line growth averages are anything to go by.

On a 14.6x P/E for 2027E, the company's implied fair value comes to no higher than $82/share, and that's to a 2027E result with an EPS of above $5.6, compared to today's $4.33. At a 5-year average, the company's 20.3x forward P/E comes to less than 7% per year.

So, in terms of forecasts and historical results, no matter if you use the premium or the 20-year average, the company's projected returns would not meet my hurdle for returns. If you have a hurdle that's less than 7%, I would go ahead and look at some alternatives - you may be seeing yourself and your returns short.

I'm willing to give the company a premium, based on fundamentals, a strong market, strong management, and a strong business model. Just not 20x P/E. Based on a continued 2-3% annual revenue growth organically, and the savings upside winding down, which makes me estimate around 5-7% EPS growth for the longer term, I would put the company's forward P/E at 17.5x. This also assumes that the company will reach over 26% Operating margin by the end of the decade.

Based on a 17.5x P/E on a forward basis, that gives us a 2027E fair value of $100/share - but that is not our PT. We want an upside to that price. To get that upside, and 15% annualized RoR, I believe we have to invest at $85/share or below, which also becomes my updated price target. The company outperformed my initial expectations, which is why I am upping the target by over 20% after 2 years.

This overvaluation also applies if you look at what peers the company can be argued to have. Depending on what you want to compare to, I would argue that some companies that are similar are Danaher (DHR), IDEX (IEX), and Xylem (XYL). They all have low yields, but Danaher and IDEX are at least better-valued than Pentair, and in the case of Danaher, more than 9x the size of Pentair in terms of market capitalization.

I would therefore also say that from a peer perspective, these are more attractive to look at.

The question, therefore, becomes if it's time to rotate Pentair as an investment.

I say yes, it's time to rotate Pentair. At anything close to $100/share, you have the company trading at its 2027E fair value. Due to the tariff uncertainty and the state of geopolitics at this time, I do not view the downside as protected enough to where I can justify sitting on a 100 %+ RoR and not act.

As of this article, I am therefore saying that I am selling my stake in Pentair after a 2-3 year investment time, realizing 100%+ returns, and waiting for a more attractive entry.

My thesis for Pentair is now as follows.

Thesis

  • Pentair is a quality company in the business of fluid filtering, pool solutions, and other various sorts of water and processing solutions and systems. The company is a market leader in key segments and is in the midst of a transformation. The drawbacks are a low yield, a low credit rating, and forecast uncertainties.
  • In 2022, I had a positive stance and made a 0.2% investment in Pentair, which since that time has returned over 100,% inclusive of dividends. That makes this a superb investment. However, the time has come to review the fundamentals and potential of the investment.
  • As of Q1'25, I view the company's valuation as too high for a continued investment, or to continue to hold on to the profits in my investment. I am therefore downgrading my rating to "HOLD", and I am taking home profits from the investment. Due to only 6-7% annualized RoR potential conservatively (as I see it), I view the company as not attractive enough here. My entry price target is increased to a PT of $85/share.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of $85/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of June 2025.
